name: Kobo Daishi
description: Kobo Daishi, also known as Kukai, was a Japanese Buddhist monk, civil servant, scholar, poet, and artist who founded the Shingon school of Buddhism. Born in 774 AD in present-day Zentsuji, Kagawa Prefecture, he displayed exceptional intellect from a young age. He initially studied Confucianism but later embraced Buddhism, seeking enlightenment through rigorous practice and study. At the age of 31, he traveled to China to study esoteric Buddhism, where he mastered Sanskrit and various Buddhist texts. Upon his return to Japan, he established Kongobuji Temple on Mount Koya, which became the center of Shingon Buddhism. Kobo Daishi is credited with numerous achievements, including the invention of the kana syllabary, improving irrigation systems, establishing schools, and promoting arts and culture. He is revered as a saint and a cultural hero in Japan, with countless legends surrounding his life and teachings. His legacy continues to influence Japanese Buddhism, art, and culture, and he remains a symbol of wisdom, compassion, and innovation. He is often depicted with a staff and a treasure vase, symbolizing his journey and his ability to bring forth abundance. His teachings emphasize the interconnectedness of all things and the potential for enlightenment in this lifetime.
subtitle: Founder of Shingon Buddhism
companions: Sometimes associated with deities he venerated.

keyMyths.title: Founding of Kongobuji Temple
keyMyths.description: Kobo Daishi is said to have thrown his vajra from China, which landed on Mount Koya, leading him to establish the temple there. The location was chosen because it was deemed spiritually significant and suitable for establishing the Shingon school's center. This myth highlights his spiritual power and the divine guidance he received.

keyMyths.title: Discovery of Hot Springs
keyMyths.description: Many hot springs in Japan are attributed to Kobo Daishi, who is said to have discovered them while traveling throughout the country. These hot springs are believed to have healing properties and are often associated with his compassionate nature and desire to help others. This myth reflects his role as a benefactor and healer.

keyMyths.title: Entering Eternal Meditation
keyMyths.description: According to tradition, Kobo Daishi did not die but entered a state of eternal meditation in his mausoleum on Mount Koya. He is believed to be still alive and continues to support those who seek his help. This myth underscores his enduring presence and the belief in his continued spiritual influence.

sources.author: Hakeda, Yoshito S.
